did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed ScNet, a lightweight deep learning model (CNN) using a MobileNetV3 backbone with depthwise separable convolutions and Squeeze-and-Excitation modules, for the classification of dermoscopic skin lesions. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset to classify seven lesion categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a new lightweight CNN model (ScNet) for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology (MobileNetV3 backbone, Squeeze-and-Excitation modules, Adaptive Classification Module), describes the dataset (HAM10000), reports specific performance metrics (97.05% accuracy, macro F1-score of 89.91%), and includes ablation studies and baseline comparisons, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: ScNet: a lightweight CNN with depthwise and SE modules for skin lesion classification
journal: Computer Methods in Biomechanics and Biomedical Engineering: Imaging & Visualization
brief_summary: This study developed ScNet, a lightweight deep learning model for classifying dermoscopic skin lesions into seven categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The architecture integrates a MobileNetV3 backbone with depthwise separable convolutions and Squeeze-and-Excitation modules, topped with an Adaptive Classification Module to balance accuracy and computational efficiency. The model was trained and evaluated using a strict lesion-wise split protocol to ensure patient-independent validation. ScNet achieved a test accuracy of 97.05%, with macro precision, recall, and F1-scores of 88.68%, 91.41%, and 89.91%, respectively. The results demonstrate that ScNet provides competitive performance while maintaining a compact architecture suitable for deployment in resource-limited environments. Although performance on underrepresented classes like dermatofibroma was lower, the model shows high potential for AI-assisted dermatology and melanoma triage.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Support: 65 images in test set.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Support: 103 images in test set.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Support: 220 images in test set.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Identified as an underrepresented class with lower performance. Support: 23 images in test set.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. High sensitivity emphasized for triage potential. Support: 223 images in test set.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Majority class with highest support. Support: 1341 images in test set.
One of the seven lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Support: 28 images in test set.

all_task_types.target: Seven dermoscopic skin lesion categories (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant, and specific lesion types) rather than continuous values. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC for class discrimination.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset containing 10,015 dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, collected from multiple sources (including ISIC archive, Vienna General Hospital, etc.) and curated by Tschandl et al. (2018). It includes lesion identifiers linking multiple images of the same lesion but no patient identifiers.

use.training: Used for training the ScNet model via lesion-wise 5-fold cross-validation on the development split (augmented images).
use.validation: Used for model selection and hyperparameter tuning via lesion-wise 5-fold cross-validation on the development split.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation on a strict lesion-wise 20% hold-out test set (original images only) to ensure patient-independent validation.
use.other: Used for baseline comparisons with other CNN architectures (ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121, MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-B0).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and baseline comparisons to ensure patient-independent validation.
Used for final evaluation of the ScNet model to ensure patient-independent validation on original images not used during training or model selection.
Used as the primary evaluation metric to avoid dominance by majority classes (e.g., NV) and to assess performance across all seven lesion categories equally.
Used to report overall performance accounting for class imbalance in the dataset.
Used to analyze performance on individual lesion categories, particularly to highlight lower performance on underrepresented classes like Dermatofibroma (DF).
Used to visualize and analyze misclassification patterns between specific lesion types (e.g., DF vs BKL/NV).
Used to assess the contribution of individual architectural components (SE modules, ACM depth, loss function, label smoothing, dropout) to the overall macro-F1 score.
Used to assess class-wise discrimination ability and compute Area Under the Curve (AUC) and Average Precision for each class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and ablation studies without statistical significance testing.

baseline_comparison_5_fold_cv.description: Baseline CNNs trained from scratch using lesion-wise 5-fold cross-validation. Macro-averaged metrics reported.

scnet_final_test_set.description: ScNet evaluated on a lesion-wise 20% hold-out test set (original images only). Macro-averaged metrics reported as primary.

auc_and_ap.description: AUC and Average Precision (AP) from ROC and Precision-Recall curves (one-vs-rest strategy).

ablation_study_cv.description: Ablation experiments on ScNet variants using lesion-wise 5-fold cross-validation. Macro-F1 and DF F1 reported.

lightweight_analysis.description: Comparison of parameter count, FLOPs, and inference latency on NVIDIA RTX 2060 (batch size=1).

architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV3 backbone initialized with ImageNet weights; transfer learning with warm-up phase (backbone frozen for 20 epochs) followed by end-to-end fine-tuning.

architecture_details.backbone: MobileNetV3-Large (width = 1.0) with depthwise separable convolutions and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) modules (reduction ratio r = 4) in selected bottleneck blocks. Uses ReLU and Hard-Swish activations. Downsampling with stride s = 2 in designated bottlenecks. Pre-pool channel count C ≈ 960.
architecture_details.classification_head: Adaptive Classification Module (ACM). Consists of Global Average Pooling (GAP), followed by two fully connected layers (512 units and 128 units) with ReLU activation, Batch Normalization, and Dropout (p = 0.5). Final layer is a 7-way softmax for classes AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC.

training_details.learning_rate: Discriminative learning rates: 1e-4 for backbone, 5e-4 for ACM during fine-tuning. ACM-only phase used 1e-3. Cosine decay schedule with 5-epoch warm-up.
training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to development split (training/validation) only; not applied to test set.
training_details.validation_protocol: Lesion-wise 5-fold cross-validation for model selection; lesion-wise 20% hold-out test set for final evaluation.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ConvNeXt and Transformer blocks hybrid model
hybrid deep learning model (Gulzar et al. 2025)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied to development split (training/validation) only: random rotation (-20 to +20 degrees), horizontal and vertical flips (p=0.5 each), scale/zoom (0.9–1.1), translation (up to 5% of width/height), colour jitter (brightness/contrast/saturation ±0.15, hue ±0.02), Gaussian noise (σ between 5 and 15), CLAHE (clip limit 2.0, tile grid 8×8, p=0.2)
Random resized crop to 224 × 224 with scale (0.8–1.0) and aspect ratio (0.9–1.1)
Image normalization to ImageNet mean and variance
Class balancing via over-sampling minority classes through augmentation and under-sampling NV prior to augmentation
Test set consisted of original images only (no augmentation or test-time augmentation)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Brightness/contrast/saturation adjusted by ±0.15; hue adjusted by ±0.02.
Clip limit 2.0, tile grid 8×8, applied with probability p = 0.2.
Target size 224 × 224; scale range 0.8–1.0; aspect ratio range 0.9–1.1.
Over-sampling of minority classes through augmentation and under-sampling of the majority class (NV) prior to augmentation.

conclusions: ScNet, a lightweight CNN using a MobileNetV3 backbone with Squeeze-and-Excitation modules and an Adaptive Classification Module, achieved a test accuracy of 97.05% and a macro F1-score of 89.91% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The model demonstrates competitive performance while maintaining a compact architecture (3.53 M parameters, ~0.23 GMACs), making it suitable for deployment in resource-limited environments.

ScNet maintained high overall accuracy and melanoma sensitivity, but performance on underrepresented classes, specifically Dermatofibroma (DF), was lower (F1-score of 0.68) due to limited sample support.

Ablation studies confirmed that the Adaptive Classification Module (ACM) is the key architectural novelty, improving macro-F1 from 0.72 to 0.75 and DF F1 from 0.61 to 0.68 compared to baseline configurations.
The study utilized a strict lesion-wise split protocol to ensure patient-independent validation, avoiding data leakage between training and testing sets.
External validation on independent cohorts is acknowledged as a necessary future step to confirm generalization beyond the HAM10000 dataset.
important_remarks: Performance on the underrepresented class Dermatofibroma (DF) was significantly lower (F1-score 0.68) due to limited sample support (n=23 in test set), indicating potential bias against rare lesions.
External validation on independent cohorts is acknowledged as a necessary future step; the study currently lacks generalization evidence beyond the HAM10000 dataset.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons relied on descriptive metrics and ablation studies.
The study did not report calibration metrics (e.g., Expected Calibration Error), which are critical for clinical decision support reliability.

Data leakage risks were mitigated by a strict lesion-wise split protocol ensuring patient-independent validation, but the dataset source (HAM10000) may contain demographic biases inherent to its collection.

The model's 'lightweight' claim is supported by parameter count (3.53 M) and FLOPs (~0.23 GMACs), but inference latency was only reported on a specific hardware (NVIDIA RTX 2060), limiting generalizability of deployment claims.
Class imbalance was addressed via augmentation and sampling, but the majority class (NV) still dominated the test set support (n=1341), potentially inflating weighted metrics.
